props.covers: The data model at the core of the interactive canvas engine: the document schema, the typed action vocabulary, the single reducer that mutates it, and the pure geometry helpers it relies on.

text.insert: The model layer is the data core of

: a FigJam-parity, typed-action, world-space interactive canvas engine, extracted into its own git submodule. It defines what a canvas document is (schema), the closed vocabulary of ways it can change (actions), the single function that performs every change (the reducer), and a small set of pure geometry helpers those changes lean on. Everything above this layer — pointer interaction, connector routing, rendering, the chrome UI, the editor shell — reads and writes through these types and functions rather than inventing its own notion of canvas state.

world-space polyline override that wins over auto-routing when present.

That's 17 object types in total. The D16 wave (document, person, database, chat) expanded the vocabulary for reference-style architecture diagrams; the W2 wave (section, pill, arrow-shape, predefined-process, code-block, chip-icon) added FigJam-parity sections plus the V2 Flow shape set, in step with the engine's move toward pixel-level FigJam fidelity.
Color resolution: tone, palette token, section tint
is the base semantic bucket (neutral, input, process, decision, memory, agent, warning, annotation) that most object types resolve their default fill/stroke from.
(D16) is a coarser color-as-meaning preset — process=blue, input=green, hot=orange/red, memory=purple, note=yellow — and wins over
(W4) wins over both, letting the FigJam pastel-pair palette apply directly.
objects sit outside this ladder entirely: their fill/chip color comes from a separate
family (green, purple, orange, yellow, gray, white, pink, red, blue, teal) resolved against FigJam's own section color tokens, not the tone/paletteToken system.
is a hand-written validator over an
input — no schema library, just explicit type-guard functions per field. It returns a discriminated
. The key distinction is soft vs. hard: an unknown
is just dropped with a warning and normalization continues, while a missing geometry, a duplicate id, an unknown object type, or a section missing its
is a hard error that fails the whole document.
is the one place that turns a failed validation into a thrown error, for callers that want fail-fast semantics instead of handling the result union themselves.
, is a single discriminated union with exactly 22 variants, every one prefixed
. This closed vocabulary is the entire surface area through which a canvas document can change — nothing mutates a document by any other path. Alongside it,
select, hand, container, process, decision, text, sticky, source-node, annotation-marker, annotation
section, pill, arrow-shape, predefined-process, code-block, chip-icon
— replaces the current selection (none / objects / connection / annotation / region).
— creates one new object of a given type, with default geometry/tone/shape if not supplied, and selects it.
— bulk-inserts objects (and optional connections) in one history entry, remapping ids on collision; used for paste.
— patches one object's fields (geometry is re-snapped, style is shallow-merged).
— bulk geometry patch for many objects at once (drag/section-carry), with an opt-out of history recording for live-preview frames.
— sets one object's width/height, snapped unless opted out.
— nudges every selected object by (dx, dy), snapped unless opted out.
— clones the selected objects (and connections between them) offset by +24/+24, and selects the clones.
— removes the current selection (objects, or the one selected connection) and any connections/links/annotations that referenced it.
— creates a connection between two existing objects, optionally with anchors and a precise relative
— patches a connection's fields, validating that any new endpoints reference real objects and aren't self-loops.
— drag-to-connect gesture: connects to an existing object under the drop point, or creates a new process object at the drop point and connects to it, in one history entry.
— attaches a note or agent-request annotation to an object, connection, or region.
— shape-swaps an object to a different type in place, preserving geometry/label/parentId while re-deriving
and seeding or clearing section-only title/tint fields.
— reparents objects into a container (or clears parent), rejecting cycles and non-container targets.
— aligns selected objects along an edge or center axis, via
— spaces three or more selected objects evenly along an axis.
— resizes a container to the padded bounding box of its children.
— re-derives every link's resolved/stale/missing/unresolved status against a set of known and stale paths.
— pops the most recent document off history.past, pushing the current document onto history.future.
— the inverse of undo, replaying a previously undone document.
— replaces the entire state with a fresh document, clearing history and selection.
Most actions record a history entry via an internal
(source, human-readable summary, and the specific object/connection/annotation ids touched) — this is what backs both the undo ledger and any "what just changed" highlight in a consuming UI.
is the one function that turns
into a new state, and it is the only place a document is ever mutated. Interactive UI gestures — drags, resizes, connector drops, keyboard nudges, all wired up in
values that an external agent or automation script would dispatch to make the identical change programmatically. Neither path has a shortcut around the other: a pointer drag and an agent's

call are, to the reducer, indistinguishable inputs.

This single choke point is what guarantees two properties hold regardless of an action's origin: consistent undo/history (every mutating action goes through the same
bookkeeping, so a human edit and an agent edit undo identically), and connector-waypoint reconciliation (every action that can move or resize an object flows through the same post-action

pass — see below — so no caller can accidentally leave a connection's explicit waypoints stale). Splitting the reducer, or giving any caller a way to patch the document object directly, would let these guarantees diverge quietly between human and agent-driven edits.

props.title: Waypoint reconciliation rides inside the reducer

text.insert: ) with a stale-waypoint choke point: after any action that changed the document (excluding undo/redo/reset, which restore stored documents verbatim),

compares each explicitly-waypointed connection's two endpoint owners before and after. If both owners translated by the same delta with no resize (a rigid move — a multi-select drag, a nudge, a section carrying its members), every waypoint shifts by that same delta, preserving trunk-and-branch fan shapes. If the owners moved asymmetrically or either was resized, the waypoints are dropped so the connector falls back to obstacle-avoiding auto-routing — FigJam's own re-route-on-asymmetric-move behavior. Because this runs inside the single reducer choke point, the waypoint change shares the triggering action's own history entry: one undo restores both geometry and waypoints together.
is a set of pure, DOM-free geometry functions operating purely on plain numbers and
at a 16px grid), anchor-point resolution for connection endpoints (
), bounding-box math over one or many geometries (
), align/distribute layout math, container-fit sizing, and stable id generation (

is the FigJam section-capture algorithm: it computes which objects a section "carries" when dragged purely from geometric overlap (an object is captured once its overlap with the section's bounds reaches a threshold fraction of the object's own area) — never via persisted
membership. Capture is recursive: a captured nested section pulls in its own captured members transitively, so dragging an outer section carries everything nested inside it.
is a type-only vendored copy of
) originally defined in the host Spectre application's own docs-model. It exists here purely so this package has zero runtime dependency back on that host app: the canvas engine only needs the
validator, which stays host-side. The file's own comment is explicit that the two copies must be kept in sync by hand if either evolves — there is no build-time link between them, by design.
There's a fitting irony here: this very doc.json's own
span attributes — the ones wrapping every
shape. The one cross-reference type this package vendors to stay decoupled from its host is the same type structuring the prose that documents it.
— whether triggered by pointer interaction, keyboard shortcuts, the chrome UI, or an external agent — must be expressed as a
. Do not hand-roll ad hoc state mutations elsewhere (e.g. mutating

directly, or writing a parallel update path that bypasses the reducer) — doing so silently breaks undo/redo history and skips the waypoint-reconciliation pass, producing state that looks fine until the next drag or undo exposes the drift.
, following the project-wide convention of keeping tests next to the source they exercise rather than in a separate top-level test tree. Three files cover this layer:

(the largest file, exercising document validation and the full action/reducer vocabulary — object and connection CRUD, selection, undo/redo, alignment/distribution, container fitting, link status resolution),
— normalization during validation and the rigid-translate vs. drop-on-asymmetric-move reconciliation behavior in the reducer), and
(the W2 wave's section capture/tint/title validation and the new V2 Flow shape vocabulary — pill, arrow-shape, predefined-process, code-block, chip-icon).
covers how pointer gestures — drag, resize, connector-drop, section-carry — get translated into the
values this document describes, and how
dispatches them through the same reducer an external agent would use.
